source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 13.1.1 MAC and IPv4 classes (pp. 222-225)

questions.q: What is a MAC address and how big is it?
options: A physical (hardware) identifier assigned at the factory, 6 bytes, usually written in hexadecimal
questions.why: The MAC address is a 6-byte factory-assigned hardware identifier, e.g. 5E:FF:56:A2:AF:15.

questions.q: What do the first three bytes of a MAC address represent?
options: The manufacturer identifier (OUI - Organizationally Unique Identifier)
questions.why: The first 3 bytes are the OUI (manufacturer); the last 3 are the manufacturer-allocated serial number.

questions.q: Which range corresponds to a Class A IPv4 address?
questions.why: Class A is 0-127, Class B 128-191, Class C 192-223, Class D 224-239, Class E 240-255 (first octet).

questions.q: What are Class D addresses (224.0.0.0-239.255.255.255) used for?
options: Multicast communication (not assigned to individual hosts)
questions.why: Class D is for multicast; Class E (240-255) is reserved and not used for regular networks.

questions.q: Which classes have their own private address ranges?
questions.why: Classes A, B and C each have private ranges; 0.0.0.0 is reserved and not assigned to a host.
